name: Alaska Office of Information Technology (OIT)
tagline: Alaska's consolidated central IT: OIT, within the Department of Administration, runs the state's enterprise infrastructure, cloud, security, and shared services, and sets statewide technology policy under the State CIO.
overview: The Alaska Office of Information Technology (OIT) is the state's consolidated central IT organization, sitting within the Department of Administration (DOA) and led by the State Chief Information Officer. Established by administrative order to centralize technology that had been scattered across departments, OIT operates Alaska's enterprise infrastructure — servers, licensing, networks, helpdesk, cloud platforms, and statewide applications such as myAlaska — and houses the state's cybersecurity organization under a Chief Information Security Officer. OIT sets statewide IT policy, standards, and the FY26-FY28 strategic plan, and embeds Chief Technology Officers and Department Technology Officers across agencies to deliver a 'one government' technology model. It is best understood as the enterprise relationship for selling into Alaska state government: OIT owns the central infrastructure and security direction, though large project funding and agency-specific buying still flow through the line departments and the state's budget process. Note that Alaska's marquee connectivity effort — the $1B-class BEAD broadband program — is administered separately by the Alaska Broadband Office under the Department of Commerce, not by OIT, so connectivity is context rather than an OIT procurement.

budget.intro: OIT is a centralized service organization funded largely through chargebacks and inter-agency receipts for the infrastructure, licensing, and support it delivers, rather than a single large general-fund line. Its FY2026 operating budget within the Department of Administration was roughly $64.6M, with the bulk in licensing, infrastructure, and servers. Confirm enacted figures against Alaska OMB and the Legislative Finance records before forecasting.

stats.label: OIT budget (FY2026)
stats.note: Enacted operating budget within Dept. of Administration; verify against OMB / LegFin

stats.label: Licensing, infra & servers
stats.note: Largest OIT allocation — the infrastructure/software spend most relevant to ISG/CSG

stats.label: BEAD broadband (context)
stats.note: Federal allocation administered by the Alaska Broadband Office (Dept. of Commerce), not OIT

notes: OIT's spend is heavily cost-recovered through inter-agency receipts — the state's centralized model means much of its budget passes through from the agencies it serves.
The largest IT project funding sits with line departments and flows through the Investment Review Board (IRB) and Alaska's appropriations process — track those gates for net-new opportunity.

BEAD and satellite connectivity ($629M in initial deployment awards approved March 2026, including Starlink and major fiber/wireless providers) is a separate Department of Commerce motion, not OIT procurement — relevant as ecosystem context, not a direct OIT line.

Gov. Dunleavy signed the FY2027 operating budget on June 24, 2026 (with vetoes); confirm the enacted OIT/Dept. of Administration IT lines against OMB and Legislative Finance before forecasting — OIT spend remains largely cost-recovered through inter-agency chargebacks.

initiatives.name: Cloud migration & modernization
initiatives.summary: OIT's multi-phase 'move and modernize' effort has reached 1,100+ applications on Microsoft Azure over roughly 21 months without service disruption (per a June 2026 Microsoft case study), citing dynamic disaster and ransomware recovery and elastic capacity. The Permanent Fund Dividend system is being rebuilt cloud-native (targeted go-live 2026) and ~15,000 state employees now have M365 Copilot. A durable pull for hybrid-cloud, migration services, AI-ready platforms, and the on-prem footprint that anchors a hybrid estate.

initiatives.name: myAlaska + agentic AI
initiatives.summary: OIT issued a November 2025 RFI to embed 'agentic AI' into the myAlaska mobile app, with Microsoft AI feature integration in the FY26-FY28 plan — moving from a basic portal to autonomous, multi-step transactions that interact with legacy systems. Signals demand for AI-ready compute, data platforms, and integration services.

initiatives.name: Mainframe & legacy modernization
initiatives.summary: The FY26-FY28 strategic plan includes a Mainframe Migration Project and statewide application lifecycle management. A multi-year track for migration, modern infrastructure, and the data platforms that replace legacy systems.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity: Zero Trust, managed SOC & DLP
initiatives.summary: OIT's plan advances a Zero Trust roadmap, a managed Security Operations Center, and data loss prevention / data labeling governance under the State CISO. A centrally driven security theme spanning detection, identity, and resilience for state entities.

procurement.intro: Selling into Alaska state government runs on the Department of Administration's statewide contract vehicles and cooperative agreements, not one-off RFPs. The DOA Office of Procurement and Property Management (OPPM) owns statewide master agreements, and Alaska participates in NASPO ValuePoint (formerly WSCA-NASPO) cooperative contracts, including Cloud Solutions. Confirm Dell's coverage on the relevant Alaska statewide master agreements and NASPO ValuePoint vehicles first — it is the access path to far more than OIT alone, including agencies, the University of Alaska, boroughs, cities, and school districts.

vehicles.name: Alaska statewide master agreements (OPPM)
vehicles.detail: The DOA Office of Procurement and Property Management owns the statewide master agreements for IT hardware, software, and services available to executive-branch departments and other Alaska public entities.

vehicles.name: NASPO ValuePoint (via DOA)
vehicles.detail: Alaska participates in NASPO ValuePoint cooperative master agreements — including the Cloud Solutions contract (SaaS/PaaS/IaaS) — as a national co-op route for cloud, software, and hardware.

vehicles.name: OIT central infrastructure & IRB gates
vehicles.detail: OIT delivers consolidated servers, licensing, cloud, and managed services, and major IT projects clear the Investment Review Board — the buying center for enterprise infrastructure and the checkpoint funded projects pass through.

powerStructure.name: Bill Smith
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Officer, OIT
powerStructure.note: CIO since 2019 (the state's second-ever CIO), still listed as State CIO in 2026 (e.g., Alaska Digital Government Summit 2026); ~28 years public/private IT, based in Anchorage. Sets statewide tech direction — the top enterprise relationship for the Alaska motion.

powerStructure.name: Niel Smith
powerStructure.title: Deputy CIO, OIT
powerStructure.note: Led the state's rapid Azure cloud migration (1,100+ apps); a hands-on technical decision-maker for infrastructure, modernization, and the hybrid-cloud roadmap. Key working-level relationship beneath the State CIO.

powerStructure.name: State CISO (Dept. of Administration)
powerStructure.title: Statewide cybersecurity & risk
powerStructure.note: Leads OIT's enterprise security program — strategy, standards, incident response, Zero Trust, and the managed SOC. As of mid-2026 the CISO role (PCN 02-X069) has been posted/recruited — treat the seat as open/transitioning and confirm the current officeholder before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Paula Vrana
powerStructure.title: Commissioner, Department of Administration
powerStructure.note: OIT sits within DOA; the Commissioner has publicly championed the cloud-migration modernization. The departmental executive above the CIO.

powerStructure.name: DOA Office of Procurement & Property Management (OPPM)
powerStructure.title: Statewide contracts & cooperative agreements
powerStructure.note: Owns the statewide master agreements and NASPO ValuePoint participating addenda most Alaska public buyers use — the path onto the state's contract vehicles.

powerStructure.name: OIT Investment Review Board (IRB)
powerStructure.title: IT project funding & oversight
powerStructure.note: Reviews and gates major IT investments statewide — the checkpoint large funded projects clear; track it for net-new opportunity.

news.title: OIT reaches cloud milestone: 1,100+ apps on Azure; PFD rebuilt cloud-native; ~15K staff on M365 Copilot
news.impact: A June 2026 Microsoft case study details OIT's 'move and modernize' migration of 1,100+ applications to Azure over ~21 months without disruption; the Permanent Fund Dividend system is being rebuilt cloud-native (targeted 2026) and ~15,000 employees now have M365 Copilot. Confirms a durable pull for hybrid-cloud, AI-ready platforms, and the on-prem anchor of a hybrid estate; Deputy CIO Niel Smith led the effort.

news.title: Legislative Audit solicits independent NIST/CIS cybersecurity assessment of select state IT systems (RFP 26-33-03)
news.impact: The Division of Legislative Audit — not OIT — sought a firm to review select State of Alaska IT systems against the NIST CSF and CIS Controls (proposals due April 3, 2026), signaling legislative scrutiny of the enterprise security posture OIT manages. A demand signal for security assessment, remediation, Zero Trust, and managed-SOC work.

news.title: Alaska BEAD final proposal approved — $629M in initial deployment awards
news.impact: Federal broadband funding (fiber, fixed wireless, and Starlink satellite) to 46,000+ locations, administered by the Alaska Broadband Office (Dept. of Commerce), not OIT — ecosystem context for connectivity-dependent state IT, not a direct OIT buy.

news.title: OIT plans agentic-AI overhaul of myAlaska via November 2025 RFI
news.impact: State explores autonomous AI modules that complete multi-step transactions and interact with legacy systems — demand signal for AI-ready compute, data platforms, and integration services.

news.title: OIT FY26-FY28 strategic plan: cloud, mainframe migration, Zero Trust & managed SOC
news.impact: Three-year roadmap (Enterprise Alignment → Selective Growth → Transformation) names cloud, AI enablers, mainframe migration, an Emerging Technology Center of Excellence, and a managed SOC — the blueprint for where infrastructure and security spend is headed.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's coverage on Alaska's DOA statewide master agreements and NASPO ValuePoint vehicles (incl. Cloud Solutions) — it's the access path to OIT plus agencies, the University of Alaska, boroughs, cities, and school districts.

Lead enterprise through OIT's central infrastructure: position ISG/AI-ready compute, client refresh, and the on-prem anchor of a hybrid estate against the Phase 2 cloud migration and mainframe modernization, framed as statewide standards rather than agency-by-agency.

Tie AI-infrastructure, data platforms, and integration services to the myAlaska agentic-AI effort and the FY26-FY28 Emerging Technology Center of Excellence — and align to Alaska's forming AI governance.
Engage the State CISO on the Zero Trust roadmap, managed SOC, and DLP — a centrally driven security theme; verify the current officeholder before forecasting.

Treat BEAD/satellite connectivity as ecosystem context owned by the Alaska Broadband Office (Dept. of Commerce), not OIT — use it to frame demand for resilient, edge, and disaster-recovery infrastructure rather than as a direct OIT procurement.
